questions.question: What is 'AI in the Wild' primarily concerned with?
options: Real-world AI deployments and their practical impacts
questions.explanation: AI in the Wild studies how AI systems perform in real-world conditions — deployment challenges, unexpected behaviors, societal impacts, and the gap between lab performance and production reality.

questions.question: AI systems typically perform worse in real-world deployment than in controlled benchmark tests.
questions.explanation: The lab-to-production gap is well-documented: distribution shift, edge cases, adversarial inputs, and infrastructure issues cause real-world performance to drop 10-30% below benchmark scores.

questions.question: When an AI model's performance degrades over time because the real world changes, this is called model ___.
questions.explanation: Model drift occurs when the data distribution shifts over time (seasonality, behavior changes, market shifts). Models trained on historical data become stale. Requires monitoring and periodic retraining.

questions.question: A facial recognition system deployed at airport security has 99.9% accuracy on the test set but produces 100 false arrests per day (1M daily passengers). Should it be kept?

scenario.context: 100 million annual passengers. False positive rate: 0.01% (seems great).

options: Keep it — 99.9% accuracy is excellent and it catches real threats
Remove it — 100 innocent people wrongly flagged daily is unacceptable
Add human review for all flagged individuals before any action
Keep but adjust threshold to reduce false positives (at cost of more false negatives)
questions.explanation: The base rate problem: even tiny false positive rates produce many false alarms in high-volume systems. Human-in-the-loop review is essential — the AI flags, humans decide. No fully automated arrests should occur.

questions.question: Match each AI failure mode to its real-world example:

right: Self-driving car fails in snow (never in training data)
AI game bot finds exploit to score points without playing correctly
Stop sign with stickers causes misclassification
Pilots ignore their own judgment because autopilot says otherwise

questions.explanation: Each represents a distinct failure: new conditions (shift), gaming the metric (reward hack), intentional manipulation (adversarial), and over-trusting AI (automation bias). All are documented in real deployments.

questions.question: Should companies be required to disclose when AI makes decisions that significantly affect people's lives?
questions.scenario: AI is used for: hiring (resume screening), lending (credit decisions), healthcare (diagnosis), and criminal justice (recidivism prediction). Most affected individuals don't know AI was involved.

options.label: Mandatory disclosure for all AI-assisted decisions
options.framework: Transparency — people have the right to know

options.label: Only for adverse decisions (rejections, denials)
options.framework: Minimal burden — disclose when it matters

options.label: No requirement — the decision quality matters, not the method
Disclosure + right to human review (algorithmic due process)

questions.explanation: The EU AI Act and proposed US legislation converge on disclosure + contestability for high-risk AI decisions. People should know AI was involved AND have the ability to request human review of adverse decisions.
